claim: Ethereum very likely already satisfied the contract’s $2,400 trigger on the governing venue during the Apr 13-19 ET window, so this should be treated as an almost-settled Yes driven by Binance-specific resolution mechanics rather than a live directional ETH bet.

frontmatter.contract_ambiguity_reason: Exact qualifying Binance 1m candle was not archived from the final settlement surface despite direct API verification.
independently_verified_points: Polymarket rules name Binance ETH/USDT 1m candle High as the resolution source
The contract window is Apr 13 12:00 AM ET through Apr 19 11:59 PM ET
Direct Binance data in the checked window showed a high of 2415.5 above the 2400 threshold
Risk-manager artifacts captured a max observed 1m high of 2415.5 at 2026-04-14 10:32 ET
frontmatter.verification_gap_summary: The remaining gap is archival proof from the exact final Binance 1m settlement surface rather than API-derived verification alone.
frontmatter.best_countercase_summary: The only serious surviving countercase is a settlement-path mismatch between Binance API verification and the exact data surface Polymarket/UMA would honor.
frontmatter.main_reason_for_disagreement: Remaining disagreement is mostly about how much source-of-truth and settlement-path risk to haircut after a >2400 Binance print was found.
frontmatter.resolution_mechanics_summary: Yes resolves if any Binance ETH/USDT 1m candle High reaches 2400 or more during Apr 13-19 ET.
frontmatter.freshness_driver: Short-dated threshold market with resolution tied to Binance 1m highs and any late dispute or correction in market state.
decision_blockers: No major factual blocker remains if Binance API data is accepted as operative evidence
Residual auditability gap from not archiving the exact qualifying Binance 1m candle from the final settlement surface
Small tail risk of settlement-path or data-surface inconsistency
frontmatter.next_checkpoint: Confirm no Polymarket/UMA dispute or reversal after the already-observed qualifying Binance print.

sections.Alpha summary: Market implied probability was 0.916. My final syndicated range is 0.97 to 0.995. The edge versus market looks actionable but modest rather than huge, because the main thesis is that the trigger was already hit on the named venue. The likely mispricing is that the assignment snapshot lagged a near-settled state while Binance-specific threshold evidence already existed.
sections.Input coverage: All five personas were available and usable. Coverage is complete. I critically checked the raw findings against sidecars and found the sidecars broadly faithful; the only meaningful incompleteness was variant-view, which underweighted evidence because it failed to recover the exact rules text that other lanes did recover. Supporting source notes were used materially for verification, especially the Polymarket rules note and Binance verification notes.
sections.Market-implied baseline: Baseline market-implied probability at dispatch was 0.916. Several lanes also found later page/state evidence implying the market had effectively moved to 1.0 after the qualifying print, so the 0.916 snapshot appears stale relative to later verification.
sections.Syndicated probability estimate: Final post-synthesis estimate: 0.97 to 0.995. I do not go to literal certainty because the last residual risk is operational rather than directional: settlement-path mismatch, data-surface discrepancy, or an unusual dispute. But after synthesis-stage review, this is much closer to already-met than merely likely-to-happen.
sections.Difference from swarm-implied center: This is below the swarm median/center near 0.99 only slightly, not materially. I keep a bounded 0.97 to 0.995 range rather than collapsing to near-100 because large claimed edges should be independently verified, and the final archival chain still lacks a belt-and-suspenders capture of the exact qualifying Binance minute candle on the ultimate settlement surface. That said, the risk-manager lane’s direct 1m verification is strong enough that I did not compress meaningfully toward the 0.916 market.
sections.Agreement or disagreement with market: I modestly disagree above the 0.916 assignment snapshot, but mostly because that snapshot appears behind the true state of evidence. Relative to the live later market state several lanes observed, I roughly agree: the contract looks effectively satisfied already. So the synthesis says the market was directionally right and, if anything, initially still a bit too low.
sections.Independent verification of edge: Independent verification was strong. I independently rely on two distinct functions: Polymarket rule text for what counts, and Binance direct market data for whether it happened. Most importantly, the risk-manager source note records paginated Binance 1m klines over the relevant window and a max observed high of 2415.5 at 2026-04-14 10:32 ET. That is stronger than the looser 24h ticker and 1h-kline checks used in some other lanes. What remains unverified is not the threshold crossing itself so much as archival capture from the exact final settlement-facing display path. That is why verification quality is high, not absolute.
sections.Compression toward market due to verific: No meaningful compression toward market was needed. The provisional swarm edge versus 0.916 was about 7.4 points, which ordinarily would warrant skepticism. But the synthesis-stage review found enough independent support: explicit Polymarket rules plus direct Binance 1m evidence above threshold inside the window. The only surviving uncertainty is procedural tail risk, not a missing core fact.
sections.Timing and catalyst posture: The key catalyst already appears to have occurred: a qualifying Binance print above 2400 on Apr 14. From here the edge should decay toward certainty rather than widen, unless a dispute or data-path anomaly appears. Waiting probably does not improve the decision much except for confirming no settlement reversal.
sections.Decision blockers: There is no major blocker to a high-confidence Yes interpretation. The only caution flags are minor: no archived screenshot/export from the exact final settlement surface, small risk of Binance API versus UI discrepancy, and small risk of an unusual Polymarket/UMA dispute.
sections.Implication for the question: For the actual market question, the synthesis implication is that Ethereum has already reached the relevant threshold under the contract’s operative rules, so Yes is the downstream-ready answer barring an operational resolution anomaly.
sections.Consensus across personas: Strong consensus that this is a source-of-truth market, not a broad ETH-direction market. Strong consensus that Polymarket rules point to Binance ETH/USDT 1m candle highs during the Apr 13-19 ET window. Strong consensus that Binance showed a print above 2400, with several lanes citing 2415.5. Strong consensus that residual risk, if any, is settlement mechanics rather than future price path.
sections.Key disagreements across personas: Main disagreement was interpretive/weighting-based rather than factual. Variant-view assigned more weight to unresolved source-of-truth ambiguity because it failed to recover the exact rules text and therefore stayed at 0.84. Market-implied and base-rate used weaker verification artifacts than risk-manager in places, relying on 1h or 24h Binance summaries, which makes those lanes slightly more fragile than their confidence suggested. There was also a mild timing/state disagreement about whether the relevant market snapshot was 0.916 or already effectively 1.0 after later checks.
sections.Best countercase: Best surviving countercase came from variant-view: the market may be somewhat overconfident if the exact settlement source or data surface differs from the Binance evidence captured in the run. After checking the raw findings, I think that countercase is preserved but weakened substantially by the risk-manager lane’s direct 1m Binance verification.
sections.Encapsulated assumptions: Shared assumptions: Polymarket’s recovered rules text is operative; Binance API data is representative of the named settlement venue; ET window conversion was handled correctly. Contested assumptions: whether 1h or 24h Binance data is enough versus exact 1m candles; whether page-source resolved state should be trusted as fully current. Fragile assumptions: no exceptional dispute or settlement override occurs.
sections.Encapsulated evidence map: Strongest supporting evidence: explicit Polymarket rules naming Binance ETH/USDT 1m Highs; Binance direct data showing highs of 2415.5; risk-manager note identifying a max observed 1m high at 2026-04-14 10:32 ET. Strongest contradictory evidence: no direct contradiction on price, only the methodological concern that some lanes relied on 1h or 24h summaries rather than exact 1m archival proof. Authoritative source-of-truth evidence: Polymarket rules page plus Binance venue data. Mixed evidence: CoinGecko and other non-governing venue context, which diverged and therefore was correctly downweighted.
sections.Evidence weighting: I gave highest weight to the Polymarket rules note and the risk-manager Binance 1m verification note. I gave medium weight to the market-implied lane’s 1h Binance kline evidence and the base-rate lane’s 24h ticker evidence because they support but do not fully complete the minute-level requirement. I heavily downweighted generic contextual feeds and any implication from ETH broad strength alone, because the contract is venue- and interval-specific.
sections.Counterpoints / strongest disconfirming: The strongest disconfirming evidence is procedural, not directional: if the exact Binance 1m series or chart surface used in final settlement were to differ from the API-derived verification, or if a timezone/window mapping error were discovered, the confidence would be overstated. No lane produced credible evidence that Binance stayed below 2400 in-window.
sections.Resolution or source-of-truth interpreta: The contract mechanics are fairly clear. The relevant question is not whether ETH closed above 2400, nor whether another exchange printed 2400, nor whether an aggregator showed it. The market resolves Yes if any Binance ETH/USDT 1-minute candle during Apr 13 00:00 ET through Apr 19 23:59 ET has a final High at or above 2400. On the evidence reviewed, that condition appears satisfied.
sections.Why this could create or destroy alpha: The alpha angle is mostly about recognizing that this was a rules-mechanics contract, not a live macro ETH forecast. Anyone pricing it as a generic weekly ETH momentum question could understate a near-settled Yes after the qualifying wick already printed. On the other hand, alpha gets destroyed if one overstates edge without verifying the named venue and candle interval. This case rewards source-of-truth literacy more than market narrative.
sections.What would falsify this interpretation /: A direct audit showing no Binance ETH/USDT 1m candle high at or above 2400 during the qualifying ET window would change the view materially. So would an official Polymarket/UMA clarification excluding the observed print, a documented API-versus-settlement-surface mismatch, or an actual dispute/reversal of the apparent resolution state.
sections.Highest-value next research: If any further work were desired, the single highest-value check would be to archive the exact qualifying Binance 1m candle from the precise settlement-facing data surface or an official Polymarket/UMA confirmation tied to that print. But this is optional rather than necessary for the current handoff.
sections.Source-quality assessment: Primary source class relied on most: Polymarket governing rules plus Binance direct market data. Most important secondary source class: non-governing contextual price feeds only as a sanity check. Evidence independence is medium to medium-high: one source defines the rule, the other tests the factual trigger. Source-of-truth ambiguity is low-to-minor after verification. The bundle is not bottlenecked by missing personas, but some upstream lanes were more confidence-heavy than provenance-heavy.
sections.Verification impact: Yes, additional verification beyond mere cross-persona summary materially changed the synthesis. Cross-lane comparison exposed that variant-view was cautious mainly because it lacked recovered rules text, while base-rate and market-implied had slightly weaker verification chains than their confidence implied. The risk-manager lane provided the strongest synthesis-stage anchor by documenting direct Binance 1m verification, which materially strengthened the final Yes view and reduced the need to compress toward market.
sections.Persona contribution map: base-rate — framed the market correctly as a venue-specific threshold-resolution case and identified Polymarket state plus Binance confirmation as decisive, though its own Binance evidence was only 24h-summary strength. catalyst-hunter — best captured the idea that the decisive catalyst had already happened and preserved useful page-source resolved-state evidence. market-implied — correctly treated 0.916 as a prior to interrogate and explained why extreme pricing could be rational under venue-specific rules, though it leaned on 1h candles rather than the strongest 1m proof. risk-manager — contributed the most decision-relevant verification by documenting paginated Binance 1m data with a max high of 2415.5 at 2026-04-14 10:32 ET; this is the strongest lane. variant-view — preserved the real residual countercase around source-of-truth mismatch and settlement-path risk, but remained too conservative because it lacked the rules recovery and stronger direct verification found elsewhere.
sections.Reusable lesson signals: Possible durable lesson: crypto threshold markets often reduce to named-venue data mechanics, not broad directional thesis quality. Possible underbuilt driver: exchange-specific settlement-path integrity and candle-interval dependence. Possible source-quality lesson: 24h and 1h summaries are useful but should not be allowed to masquerade as exact evidence when the rule names 1m candles. Reusability confidence: high.
sections.Orchestrator review suggestions: review later for durable lesson: yes; review later for driver candidate: yes; review later for canon or linkage issue: no; review later for swarm-method issue: yes. Reason: this case highlights a recurring driver around named-venue threshold resolution and also shows a method lesson that lane confidence should track exactness of evidence surface more tightly.
sections.Recommended follow-up: No follow-up needed for current decision use beyond passive monitoring for any settlement dispute or reversal. If a retrospective audit package is wanted, collect the exact qualifying Binance 1m candle artifact.
